title: Timed OA & CCAT Strategy
summary: Modules 01–14 built your toolkit; this capstone teaches you to spend it under a clock. Mock-OA rituals, partial-credit strategy, bug-proofing habits, and the exact playbooks for Microsoft (Codility), Flipkart (GRiD), and Trilogy's CCAT.

notes.heading: The real bottleneck (it isn't knowledge)
notes.body:
Modules 01–14 built your *toolkit*. This module is about a different skill entirely: **using that toolkit fast, correctly, on the first try, while a clock runs and a scoreboard watches**. Solving a problem with unlimited time and solving it inside an OA window are as different as jogging and racing — and the OA only grades the race.

Here is the honest diagnosis. You are *not* weak at DSA. You build real things, you are a Codeforces pupil/specialist, and your arrays, hashing, stacks, and trees are genuinely solid. What actually costs you points is **executing a correct solution quickly under time pressure**, especially in the topics you have not drilled: graphs, binary-search-on-answer, heaps, harder DP and backtracking. An OA gives exactly zero credit for "I would have gotten it with 20 more minutes."

> [!KEY]
> Reframe the goal for these final weeks: not *learn more*, but **convert what you already know into fast, bug-free, tested submissions**. Speed and reliability are the deliverable — knowledge is just the raw material.

> [!NOTE]
> Everything in this module is meta-strategy: there is no new algorithm here. That is exactly why people skip it — and then donate a third of their OA score to pacing mistakes and untested edge cases that one week of drilling would have prevented.

notes.heading: The weekly mock ritual
notes.body:
The single highest-value habit before any OA season: **one full timed set per week**, plus a couple of short drills. A full set means **3 problems, 90 minutes, hard stop, no pausing, no looking anything up**. That last constraint is the whole point — the moment you allow yourself a peek at a hint, you are no longer measuring the thing the OA measures.

Your weekly template (pick a fixed day and time so it becomes a ritual):

| Session | What | Why |
|---|---|---|
| 1× full mock | 3 problems, 90 min, hard stop | your "real OA" |
| 2–3× short drills | 1 medium in 25 min, or 2 easies in 25 min | builds the solve-fast-and-move reflex |
| 1× review | re-solve every failure *from scratch* | repeat until it is automatic |

Where the sets come from:

- **LeetCode Weekly & Biweekly contests** — the closest thing to a real OA: four fresh problems, 90 minutes, live ranking. Do them live when you can.
- **LeetCode virtual contests** — run any past contest as a timed session on your own schedule. An infinite supply of realistic mocks.
- **Company mocks** — LeetCode's company-tagged lists (Microsoft, Flipkart, …) as timed blocks, plus HackerRank and Codility sample tests, so the *interface* is never a surprise on test day.

On the clock itself, budget before you code:

[[fig:m15-mock-timeline]]

- Spend the **first 3–5 minutes scanning all three problems** and ranking them by difficulty. Never just start at #1.
- **Easiest first, always.** Bank a guaranteed solve in ~15–20 minutes.
- Medium next (~30–35 min), hard last with whatever remains. Rough split: **20 / 35 / 35** — adjust as you read.

> [!EXAM] The skip-and-return rule
> If you are **10+ minutes into a problem with no clear path**, stop. Submit any partial you have and move to the next problem. A fresh problem you can fully solve is worth strictly more than a stuck one you might crack. Come back only if time remains.

> [!ANALOGY]
> Mocks are fire drills. Nobody reads the evacuation manual during an actual fire — you drill the route until your legs know it. Test-day nerves cannot rattle a routine you have already run eight times.

notes.heading: Partial scoring: brute force first, optimize second
notes.body:
Codility and HackerRank score each submission against **many hidden test groups** and award **partial credit** per group: small "correctness" groups and large "performance" groups. That changes the optimal play completely:

1. **Brute force first.** Write the obviously-correct $O(n^2)$ (or worse) solution and **submit it immediately**. It passes the correctness groups and locks in real points.
2. **Then optimize.** Improve to the target complexity to capture the performance groups.
3. You are now never at zero on a problem, and the clock is de-risked: run out of time mid-optimization and you *keep* the brute-force points.

[[fig:m15-partial-credit]]

> [!EXAM]
> On Codility, a submitted correct-but-slow solution is often worth 40–60% of the problem. An unsubmitted elegant solution is worth exactly 0%. Submit early, submit often.

> [!WARNING] Flipkart inverts this
> On Flipkart-style tests, scoring is much closer to **all-or-nothing per problem**. There, a fully correct solution to fewer problems beats partials sprayed across all three — see the company playbooks below. Know which scoring regime you are in *before* the test starts.

notes.heading: Bug-proofing: test-first habits under the clock
notes.body:
Most points lost in an OA are lost to *bugs*, not to missing algorithms. You cannot debug your way out of a 90-minute window, so build reflexes that catch bugs before the judge does.

**Sanity-test before every submit:**

- Run the **provided samples** first, obviously.
- Then hand-run **2–3 tiny cases of your own**, typed directly.
- Add one **known-answer case you computed by hand**, so you are not just trusting the sample.

**The edge-case checklist** — run it mentally on every problem:

- **Empty input**: `n == 0`, empty string, empty array.
- **Single element**: `n == 1` — loops and two-pointer logic love to break here.
- **Duplicates / all-equal** values.
- **Overflow** — the #1 silent killer (see the pitfall below).
- **Boundaries**: max constraints ($n = 10^5$, values up to $10^9$), negatives, min/max ints.
- **Sorted / reverse-sorted / already-the-answer** inputs.

> [!PITFALL] Overflow is the #1 silent killer
> The sum of $10^5$ ints each up to $10^9$ is $10^{14}$ — far past `int`'s $\approx 2.1 \times 10^9$. **Default to `long long`** for sums, products, prefix sums, and anything near $10^9$. This one habit will save you entire failed test groups.

Paste this at the top of every OA solution:

```cpp
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

int main() {
    ios_base::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);
    // ... solve
    return 0;
}
```

Without `sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr);`, reading $10^5$–$10^6$ lines with `cin` can TLE **purely on I/O**. And use `'\n'` instead of `endl` inside loops — `endl` flushes the stream on every single call.

**Avoiding TLE — read the constraints first.** They tell you the target complexity before you write a line:

[[fig:m15-constraints-map]]

> [!PITFALL] Accidental $O(n^2)$
> Watch for: repeated `string +=` in a loop, `.find()` inside a loop, erasing from the middle of a `vector`, and passing big containers **by value** (pass by `const&`). Prefer `vector` + `reserve` over repeated reallocation; `unordered_map` gives $O(1)$ lookups but carries a real constant factor.

notes.heading: Company playbooks: Microsoft, Flipkart, Trilogy
notes.body:
**Microsoft (Codility).** Problem style leans **LeetCode-medium**, with a real emphasis on **graphs and DP** — exactly your two biggest gaps, so weight your prep toward modules 10–13. Get familiar with Codility's editor and its test-group partial scoring: the brute-force-first play applies directly. Later rounds are live and interview-style, so **verbalize your approach before coding** — state the idea, the complexity, and the edge cases out loud. Interviewers score your *reasoning*, not just the green checkmark; practice narrating while you solve so it feels natural.

**Flipkart (GRiD / SDE).** The format is brutal by design: **3 hard problems in 90 minutes**, and most strong candidates do *not* full-clear it. Scoring rewards **completely correct** solutions, so read all three in the first 5 minutes, identify the 1–2 most in your wheelhouse (likely greedy / implementation / DP given your strengths), and drive them to 100% instead of spraying partial attempts. Correctness and edge cases over cleverness — this is where the module 11–13 work pays off.

**Trilogy Innovations.** Two separate things, and **both** gate you:

1. **The coding assessment** — hard, **matrix / grid / DP-heavy** problems: heavy 2D DP, grid traversal, simulation. Modules 11–13 are the prep.
2. **The CCAT (Criteria Cognitive Aptitude Test)** — a separate **15-minute, 50-question** aptitude test mixing math, logic, verbal, and spatial reasoning. It is not a coding test, and being a strong coder does **not** carry over automatically.

[[fig:m15-ccat-pacing]]

> [!EXAM] The CCAT bar
> 50 questions in 15 minutes is **~18 seconds per question**. Almost nobody finishes; the score is the raw number correct. Trilogy filters aggressively — **aim for ~45/50** and do not treat it as a formality.

How to prep the CCAT (timed sets — no paid sites needed, standard aptitude material covers all of it):

- **Fast mental math**: percentages, ratios, fractions, averages, simple/compound interest, unit conversions, number series — practiced *without* a calculator.
- **Logical / verbal reasoning**: syllogisms, if-then deductions, word analogies, odd-one-out, sentence logic.
- **Spatial reasoning**: shape rotation, folding/unfolding, pattern completion, 2D/3D visualization.
- **The meta-skill**: answer easy ones instantly, flag hard ones, never burn 40 seconds on a single question. Take full 15-min / 50-question mocks until the pace is muscle memory.

notes.heading: The 8-week map and the mindset
notes.body:
Here is the whole plan at a realistic **1–2 hr/day**, front-loading your gaps and ending in pure simulation:

| Week | Focus | Your status |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arrays & hashing, two pointers / sliding window | strength — speed warm-up, easies in under 15 min |
| 2 | **Binary search** (classic + on-answer), stacks | BS is a core gap — spend real time here |
| 3 | **Linked lists**, recursion & **backtracking** | gaps — build them clean from scratch |
| 4 | Trees & BST, tries | strength — go fast, reinforce the win |
| 5 | **Heaps**, intro **graphs** (BFS/DFS, adjacency lists) | big gaps — learn `priority_queue` cold |
| 6 | **Union-Find, topological sort, Dijkstra** | the biggest gap — most time of any week |
| 7 | **Advanced DP** (knapsack, LCS, edit distance, intervals), greedy | beyond your current 1D/2D counting |
| 8 | **Full timed simulation** — this module | 3–4 full mocks + daily CCAT sets if targeting Trilogy |

Tight on time? Compress weeks 1–2 and 4 (your strengths) and reinvest in weeks 5–7 — graphs, heaps, and advanced DP are where your points are currently being left on the table.

> [!KEY] Mindset
> **OA speed is a trainable muscle, not a talent.** Every timed set makes the next one faster; the nervousness and the fumbling both fade with reps — guaranteed. And **consistency beats intensity**: one focused set a week for eight weeks transforms you far more than one heroic 10-hour cram. Show up daily, even for 45 minutes.

> [!NOTE]
> The gaps you have — graphs, binary search, heaps, DP — are the most *learnable* topics in DSA precisely because they are pattern-based. Drill the pattern once and it is yours for every future problem. You've got this.

problems.name: Run a live LeetCode Weekly/Biweekly contest
problems.note: The closest thing to a real OA: 4 fresh problems, 90 min, live ranking — do one live whenever the schedule allows.

problems.name: Run a LeetCode virtual contest (weekly, timed)
problems.note: Any past contest as a 90-min hard-stop mock — infinite supply; no pausing, no looking things up.

problems.name: Timed company-tagged set (Microsoft / Flipkart)
problems.note: Filter by company tag, pick 3 problems, run as one 90-min block — practice the 20/35/35 budget and the skip-and-return rule.

problems.name: Codility sample test (Microsoft's platform)
problems.note: Learn the editor and the test-group partial scoring before test day — run the brute-force-first play here.

problems.name: HackerRank practice test (interface familiarity)
problems.note: Timed certification-style tests in HackerRank's real assessment UI — the interface should never be a surprise on test day.

problems.name: Short drill: 1 medium in 25 minutes
problems.note: 2–3 of these per week build the solve-fast-and-move reflex; pair with one review session re-solving every failure from scratch.

deepdives.q: **The Codility opening move** — You open a Microsoft OA on Codility: two medium problems, 70 minutes, test-group partial scoring. Problem 1: count the pairs $(i, j)$, $i < j$, with $a_i + a_j = k$, where $n \le 10^5$ and values reach $10^9$. Script your first 30 minutes: what do you write, and *when* do you submit?

**The Flipkart 5-minute triage** — Flipkart GRiD: 3 hard problems, 90 minutes, scoring close to all-or-nothing per problem. Most strong candidates do not full-clear this set. Design your first 5 minutes and the structure of the remaining 85.

**The CCAT 15-minute sprint** — Trilogy's CCAT: 50 questions in 15 minutes across math, logic, verbal, and spatial reasoning; the score is the raw number correct and the working bar is ~45/50. Design the in-test pacing policy and the two-week prep plan.
deepdives.answer:
**The arithmetic first.** $15 \times 60 / 50 = 18$ seconds per question. Almost nobody finishes all 50, and there is no partial credit per question — so the whole game is *throughput*: maximize the number of questions you answer correctly, not the elegance of any one answer.

[[fig:m15-ccat-pacing]]

**In-test policy — three passes:**

1. **Pass 1 (the sprint):** answer everything you can settle in ~15 seconds. Mental math, easy analogies, obvious series. Flag anything that smells like a 40-second grind and move — instantly, without guilt.
2. **Pass 2 (the flagged ones):** return with whatever time remains; these are now worth attempting because the cheap points are already banked.
3. **Final seconds:** the score is raw correct with no penalty, so a blank is never better than a fast guess — fill in anything still open.

> [!PITFALL]
> The classic failure mode is spending 60 seconds proving you *can* do question 7 while questions 30–50 go unseen. Three easy questions un-attempted cost more than one hard question skipped.

**Two-week prep plan (daily, alongside week-8 mocks):**

- **Fast mental math** drills: percentages, ratios, fractions, averages, simple/compound interest, unit conversions, number series — all *without* a calculator, against a timer.
- **Logical / verbal**: syllogisms, if-then deductions, word analogies, odd-one-out, sentence logic.
- **Spatial**: shape rotation, folding/unfolding, pattern completion.
- **One full 15-min / 50-question mock per day.** The mock *is* the skill: the 18-second rhythm has to become muscle memory, exactly like the 90-minute coding mock. Standard free aptitude material covers everything — no paid sites needed.

> [!KEY]
> Treat the CCAT as a first-class gate, not a formality. Trilogy filters aggressively on it, and coding skill does not transfer automatically — but the pacing skill trains in two weeks of honest timed mocks.

quiz.q: An OA problem states $n \le 10^5$ with values up to $10^9$. Your first idea is a double loop over all pairs. What should you conclude *before* writing any code?
options: $O(n^2)$ is fine — $10^{10}$ simple operations run in about a second
The target is $O(n \log n)$ or $O(n)$ — a double loop at $10^{10}$ operations will TLE the performance groups
The target must be $O(\log n)$, so only binary search can work
Constraints say nothing about complexity; just code the idea and see
quiz.explanation: Constraints are a complexity contract: $n \le 10^5$ means roughly $O(n \log n)$ or $O(n)$ for full marks, because $n^2 = 10^{10}$ operations is far beyond the ~$10^8$/sec budget. Option C over-corrects — $O(\log n)$ is the target when $n$ reaches $10^9$-scale, not $10^5$. And on a partial-credit platform the $O(n^2)$ version is still worth *submitting* — just not worth *stopping* at.

quiz.q: Codility test, 35 minutes left on a problem. You have a fully working $O(n^2)$ solution in the editor and a clear idea for the $O(n \log n)$ version. What is the best move?
options: Submit the $O(n^2)$ version now, then start on the optimized one
Skip submitting the slow version — it wastes a submission and looks bad
Move to the next problem; a slow solution is as good as no solution
Keep hand-testing the brute force for another 10 minutes before deciding
quiz.explanation: Codility awards partial credit per test group, so a correct-but-slow submission immediately banks the correctness groups (often 40–60% of the problem) and de-risks the clock: if the optimization goes wrong, you keep those points. Option B/C treat the test like all-or-nothing scoring — that logic belongs to Flipkart-style tests, not Codility.

quiz.q: Your solution reads $10^6$ integers with `cin` and prints $10^6$ lines using `endl`. The algorithm is a clean $O(n)$ pass, yet the judge reports TLE. What is the most likely fix?
options: The algorithm secretly needs to be $O(\log n)$ — rethink the approach
Call `reserve` on the vector to avoid reallocation
Replace `unordered_map` with `map` for more predictable performance
Add `sync_with_stdio(false); cin.tie(nullptr);` and print `'\n'` instead of `endl`
quiz.explanation: With a million lines, I/O itself is the bottleneck: synced `cin` is slow, and `endl` flushes the output stream on every call. The fast-I/O pair plus `'\n'` fixes both. Option A ignores that $O(n)$ is already the right complexity; B and C shave constants that are dwarfed by a million flushes.

quiz.q: You compute `int sum = 0; for (int x : a) sum += x;` where $n = 10^5$ and each element can be $10^9$. What actually happens?
options: It works — `int` comfortably holds the result
It only breaks if the array contains negative numbers
Silent overflow — the sum can reach $10^{14}$, far past `int`'s ~$2.1 \times 10^9$; use `long long`
The compiler automatically promotes the accumulation to 64-bit
quiz.explanation: The maximum sum is $10^5 \times 10^9 = 10^{14}$, while `int` tops out near $2.1 \times 10^9$. Overflow is undefined behavior and usually *silent* — samples pass, big hidden groups fail. C++ never auto-promotes an `int` accumulator, and negatives are irrelevant to the failure. Defaulting to `long long` for sums and prefix sums is the cheapest insurance in OA coding.

quiz.q: You are 12 minutes into problem 2 of a 3-problem, 90-minute set with no clear path, though a partial brute force is written. Per this module's rule, what do you do?
options: Submit the partial you have and move to the next problem, returning only if time remains
Push through — switching problems wastes the context you've built
Delete the brute force and restart the problem with a clean head
Give it exactly 5 more minutes, then decide again
quiz.explanation: The skip-and-return rule triggers at 10+ minutes with no clear path: bank any partial credit, then spend the time on a fresh problem you can fully solve — that is worth strictly more than continued staring. Option B is the sunk-cost trap that ruins OA scores, and D just re-arms the same trap with a new timer.

quiz.q: Flipkart GRiD: 3 hard problems, 90 minutes, scoring close to all-or-nothing per problem. Which plan matches how the test is actually scored?
options: Submit quick brute-force partials on all three, then improve round-robin
Split time evenly: 30 minutes per problem, in the given order
Start with the hardest while your mind is freshest
Read all three in the first 5 minutes, pick the 1–2 most in your wheelhouse, and drive them to fully correct
quiz.explanation: When scoring is all-or-nothing, three half-solutions are worth roughly nothing — full-solve beats partial, so you concentrate on the problems you can actually finish. Option A is the *Codility* strategy applied to the wrong scoring regime; B and C ignore that problem selection, not raw effort, is what the first five minutes are for.

quiz.q: CCAT: 50 questions, 15 minutes, score is the raw number correct. Question 7 looks solvable but smells like a 60-second grind. The right move?
options: Spend the 60 seconds — accuracy is what gets you to 45/50
Flag it and skip immediately — protecting the ~18 s/question pace wins more total points
Slow the whole test down; careful beats fast when only correct answers count
Skip it and every later question that even looks hard, permanently
quiz.explanation: At ~18 seconds per question, one 60-second grind silently costs you three easier questions you never reach — pace *is* the score. Option A optimizes one question at the expense of the total; C misreads "raw correct" as rewarding slowness when it actually rewards throughput; D throws away flagged questions you could win on a second pass with leftover time.
